title: Ankle

infobox.value: (informal) is the area where the [foot](/wiki/Foot "Foot") and the [leg](/wiki/Leg "Leg") meet.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The ankle includes three [joints](/wiki/Joint "Joint"): the **ankle joint proper** or **talocrural joint**, the [subtalar joint](/wiki/Subtalar_joint "Subtalar joint"), and the [inferior tibiofibular joint](/wiki/Inferior_tibiofibular_joint "Inferior tibiofibular joint").[[3]](#cite_note-Milner_1999-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Williams_&_Taunton_2007-4)[[5]](#cite_note-del_Castillo_2012_Foot_and_Ankle_Injuries-5) The movements produced at this joint are dorsiflexion and [plantarflexion](/wiki/Plantarflexion "Plantarflexion") of the foot. In common usage, the term ankle refers exclusively to the ankle region. In medical terminology, "ankle" (without qualifiers) can refer broadly to the region or specifically to the talocrural joint.[[1]](#cite_note-Moore,_Dalley_&_Agur_2013_Lower_Limb-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Region where the foot and the leg meet

The **ankle**, the **talocrural region**[[1]](#cite_note-Moore,_Dalley_&_Agur_2013_Lower_Limb-1) or the **jumping bone** (informal) is the area where the [foot](/wiki/Foot "Foot") and the [leg](/wiki/Leg "Leg") meet.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The ankle includes three [joints](/wiki/Joint "Joint"): the **ankle joint proper** or **talocrural joint**, the [subtalar joint](/wiki/Subtalar_joint "Subtalar joint"), and the [inferior tibiofibular joint](/wiki/Inferior_tibiofibular_joint "Inferior tibiofibular joint").[[3]](#cite_note-Milner_1999-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Williams_&_Taunton_2007-4)[[5]](#cite_note-del_Castillo_2012_Foot_and_Ankle_Injuries-5) The movements produced at this joint are dorsiflexion and [plantarflexion](/wiki/Plantarflexion "Plantarflexion") of the foot. In common usage, the term ankle refers exclusively to the ankle region. In medical terminology, "ankle" (without qualifiers) can refer broadly to the region or specifically to the talocrural joint.[[1]](#cite_note-Moore,_Dalley_&_Agur_2013_Lower_Limb-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The main bones of the ankle region are the [talus](/wiki/Talus_bone "Talus bone") (in the foot), the [tibia](/wiki/Tibia "Tibia"), and [fibula](/wiki/Fibula "Fibula") (both in the leg). The talocrural joint is a [synovial](/wiki/Synovial_joint "Synovial joint") [hinge joint](/wiki/Hinge_joint "Hinge joint") that connects the distal ends of the tibia and fibula in the lower limb with the proximal end of the talus.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The articulation between the tibia and the talus bears more weight than that between the smaller fibula and the talus.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure")]

### Region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Region")]

The ankle region is found at the junction of the [leg](/wiki/Human_leg "Human leg") and the [foot](/wiki/Foot "Foot"). It extends downwards ([distally](/wiki/Anatomical_terms_of_location#Proximal_and_distal "Anatomical terms of location")) from the narrowest point of the lower leg and includes the parts of the foot closer to the body (proximal) to the [heel](/wiki/Heel "Heel") and upper surface ([dorsum](/wiki/Anatomical_terms_of_location#Dorsal_and_ventral "Anatomical terms of location")) of the foot.[[8]](#cite_note-Moore_2018_p.-8): 768

### Ankle joint

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ankle joint")]

The talocrural joint is the only mortise and tenon joint in the human body,[[9]](#cite_note-Standring2015-9): 1418 the term likening the skeletal structure to the [woodworking joint](/wiki/Mortise_and_tenon "Mortise and tenon") of the same name. The bony architecture of the ankle consists of three bones: the [tibia](/wiki/Tibia "Tibia"), the [fibula](/wiki/Fibula "Fibula"), and the [talus](/wiki/Talus_bone "Talus bone"). The articular surface of the tibia may be referred to as the *plafond* ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language") for "ceiling").[[10]](#cite_note-Bucholz2012-10) The [medial malleolus](/wiki/Medial_malleolus "Medial malleolus") is a bony process extending distally off the medial tibia. The distal-most aspect of the fibula is called the [lateral malleolus](/wiki/Lateral_malleolus "Lateral malleolus"). Together, the malleoli, along with their supporting ligaments, stabilize the talus underneath the tibia.

Because the motion of the subtalar joint provides a significant contribution to positioning the foot, some authors will describe it as the lower ankle joint, and call the talocrural joint the upper ankle joint.[[11]](#cite_note-Muscolino2016-11) Dorsiflexion and Plantarflexion are the [movements](/wiki/Anatomical_terms_of_motion "Anatomical terms of motion") that take place in the ankle joint. When the foot is plantar flexed, the ankle joint also allows some movements of side to side gliding, rotation, adduction, and abduction.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The bony arch formed by the tibial plafond and the two malleoli is referred to as the ankle "[mortise](/wiki/Mortise_and_tenon "Mortise and tenon")" (or talar mortise). The mortise is a rectangular socket.[[1]](#cite_note-Moore,_Dalley_&_Agur_2013_Lower_Limb-1) The ankle is composed of three joints: the talocrural joint (also called talotibial joint, tibiotalar joint, talar mortise, talar joint), the [subtalar joint](/wiki/Subtalar_joint "Subtalar joint") (also called talocalcaneal), and the [Inferior tibiofibular joint](/wiki/Inferior_tibiofibular_joint "Inferior tibiofibular joint").[[3]](#cite_note-Milner_1999-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Williams_&_Taunton_2007-4)[[5]](#cite_note-del_Castillo_2012_Foot_and_Ankle_Injuries-5) The joint surface of all bones in the ankle is covered with articular [cartilage](/wiki/Cartilage "Cartilage").

The distances between the bones in the ankle are as follows:[[13]](#cite_note-ImaiIkoma2015-13)

* Talus - medial malleolus : 1.70 ± 0.13 mm
* Talus - tibial plafond: 2.04 ± 0.29 mm
* Talus - lateral malleolus: 2.13 ± 0.20 mm

Decreased distances indicate [osteoarthritis](/wiki/Osteoarthritis "Osteoarthritis").

### Ligaments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ligaments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/919_Ankle_Feet_Joints.jpg/250px-919_Ankle_Feet_Joints.jpg)](/wiki/File:919_Ankle_Feet_Joints.jpg)

Ligaments of ankle and feet

The ankle joint is bound by the strong [deltoid ligament](/wiki/Deltoid_ligament "Deltoid ligament") and three lateral ligaments: the [anterior talofibular ligament](/wiki/Anterior_talofibular_ligament "Anterior talofibular ligament"), the [posterior talofibular ligament](/wiki/Posterior_talofibular_ligament "Posterior talofibular ligament"), and the [calcaneofibular ligament](/wiki/Calcaneofibular_ligament "Calcaneofibular ligament").

* The *deltoid ligament* supports the medial side of the joint, and is attached at the [medial malleolus](/wiki/Medial_malleolus "Medial malleolus") of the tibia and connect in four places to the [talar shelf](/wiki/Talar_shelf "Talar shelf") of the [calcaneus](/wiki/Calcaneus "Calcaneus"), [calcaneonavicular ligament](/wiki/Calcaneonavicular_ligament "Calcaneonavicular ligament"), the [navicular tuberosity](/wiki/Navicular_tuberosity "Navicular tuberosity"), and to the medial surface of the talus.
* The *anterior* and *posterior talofibular ligaments* support the lateral side of the joint from the [lateral malleolus](/wiki/Lateral_malleolus "Lateral malleolus") of the fibula to the dorsal and ventral ends of the talus.
* The *calcaneofibular ligament* is attached at the lateral malleolus and to the lateral surface of the calcaneus.

Though it does not span the ankle joint itself, the syndesmotic ligament makes an important contribution to the stability of the ankle. This ligament spans the [syndesmosis](/wiki/Syndesmosis "Syndesmosis"), i.e. the articulation between the medial aspect of the distal fibula and the lateral aspect of the distal tibia. An isolated injury to this ligament is often called a [high ankle sprain](/wiki/High_ankle_sprain "High ankle sprain").

The bony architecture of the ankle joint is most stable in [dorsiflexion](/wiki/Dorsiflexion "Dorsiflexion").[[14]](#cite_note-14) Thus, a [sprained ankle](/wiki/Sprained_ankle "Sprained ankle") is more likely to occur when the ankle is plantar-flexed,[[15]](#cite_note-15) as ligamentous support is more important in this position. The classic ankle sprain involves the [anterior talofibular ligament](/wiki/Anterior_talofibular_ligament "Anterior talofibular ligament") (ATFL), which is also the most commonly injured ligament during [inversion](/wiki/Inversion_(kinesiology) "Inversion (kinesiology)") sprains. Another ligament that can be injured in a severe ankle sprain is the [calcaneofibular ligament](/wiki/Calcaneofibular_ligament "Calcaneofibular ligament").

### Retinacula, tendons and their synovial sheaths, vessels, and nerves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Retinacula, tendons and their synovial sheaths, vessels, and nerves")]

A number of tendons pass through the ankle region. Bands of connective tissue called *retinacula* (singular: *retinaculum*) allow the tendons to exert force across the angle between the leg and foot without lifting away from the angle, a process called bowstringing.[[11]](#cite_note-Muscolino2016-11)
The [superior extensor retinaculum of foot](/wiki/Superior_extensor_retinaculum_of_foot "Superior extensor retinaculum of foot") extends between the anterior (forward) surfaces of the tibia and fibula near their lower (distal) ends. It contains the [anterior tibial artery](/wiki/Anterior_tibial_artery "Anterior tibial artery") and [vein](/wiki/Anterior_tibial_vein "Anterior tibial vein") and the tendons of the [tibialis anterior muscle](/wiki/Tibialis_anterior_muscle "Tibialis anterior muscle") within its tendon sheath and the unsheathed tendons of [extensor hallucis longus](/wiki/Extensor_hallucis_longus_muscle "Extensor hallucis longus muscle") and [extensor digitorum longus](/wiki/Extensor_digitorum_longus_muscle "Extensor digitorum longus muscle") muscles. The [deep peroneal nerve](/wiki/Deep_peroneal_nerve "Deep peroneal nerve") passes under the retinaculum while the [superficial peroneal nerve](/wiki/Superficial_peroneal_nerve "Superficial peroneal nerve") is outside of it. The [inferior extensor retinaculum of foot](/wiki/Inferior_extensor_retinaculum_of_foot "Inferior extensor retinaculum of foot") is a Y-shaped structure. Its lateral attachment is on the calcaneus, and the band travels towards the anterior tibia where it is attached and blends with the superior extensor retinaculum. Along with that course, the band divides and another segment attaches to the [plantar aponeurosis](/wiki/Plantar_aponeurosis "Plantar aponeurosis"). The tendons which pass through the superior extensor retinaculum are all sheathed along their paths through the inferior extensor retinaculum and the tendon of the [fibularis tertius muscle](/wiki/Fibularis_tertius_muscle "Fibularis tertius muscle") is also contained within the retinaculum.

The [flexor retinaculum of foot](/wiki/Flexor_retinaculum_of_foot "Flexor retinaculum of foot") extends from the medial malleolus to the medical process of the calcaneus, and the following structures in order from medial to lateral: the tendon of the [tibialis posterior muscle](/wiki/Tibialis_posterior_muscle "Tibialis posterior muscle"), the tendon of the [flexor digitorum longus muscle](/wiki/Flexor_digitorum_longus_muscle "Flexor digitorum longus muscle"), the [posterior tibial artery](/wiki/Posterior_tibial_artery "Posterior tibial artery") and [vein](/wiki/Posterior_tibial_vein "Posterior tibial vein"), the [tibial nerve](/wiki/Tibial_nerve "Tibial nerve"), and the tendon of the [flexor hallucis longus muscle](/wiki/Flexor_hallucis_longus_muscle "Flexor hallucis longus muscle").

The [fibular retinacula](/wiki/Peroneal_retinacula "Peroneal retinacula") hold the tendons of the [fibularis longus](/wiki/Fibularis_longus "Fibularis longus") and [fibularis brevis](/wiki/Fibularis_brevis "Fibularis brevis") along the lateral aspect of the ankle region. The superior fibular retinaculum extends from the deep transverse fascia of the leg and lateral malleolus to calcaneus. The inferior fibular retinaculum is a continuous extension from the inferior extensor retinaculum to the calcaneus.[[9]](#cite_note-Standring2015-9): 1418–9

### Mechanoreceptors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Mechanoreceptors")]

Mechanoreceptors of the ankle send proprioceptive sensory input to the central nervous system (CNS).[[16]](#cite_note-16) Muscle spindles are thought to be the main type of mechanoreceptor responsible for proprioceptive attributes from the ankle.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The muscle spindle gives feedback to the CNS system on the current length of the muscle it innervates and to any change in length that occurs.

It was hypothesized that muscle spindle feedback from the ankle dorsiflexors played the most substantial role in [proprioception](/wiki/Proprioception "Proprioception") relative to other muscular receptors that cross at the ankle joint. However, due to the multi-planar range of motion at the ankle joint there is not one group of muscles that is responsible for this.[[18]](#cite_note-18) This helps to explain the relationship between the ankle and balance.

In 2011, a relationship between proprioception of the ankle and balance performance was seen in the CNS. This was done by using a fMRI machine in order to see the changes in brain activity when the receptors of the ankle are stimulated.[[19]](#cite_note-19) This implicates the ankle directly with the ability to balance. Further research is needed in order to see to what extent does the ankle affect balance.

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Function")]

Historically, the role of the ankle in locomotion has been discussed by [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") and [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"). There is no question that ankle push-off is a significant force in [human gait](/wiki/Gait_(human) "Gait (human)"), but how much energy is used in leg swing as opposed to advancing the whole-body [center of mass](/wiki/Center_of_mass "Center of mass") is not clear.[[20]](#cite_note-ZelikAdamczyk2016-20)

### Traumatic injury

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Traumatic injury")]

Of all major joints, the ankle is the most commonly injured. If the outside surface of the foot is twisted under the leg during weight bearing, the [lateral ligament](/wiki/Lateral_collateral_ligament_of_ankle_joint "Lateral collateral ligament of ankle joint"), especially the [anterior talofibular portion](/wiki/Anterior_talofibular_ligament "Anterior talofibular ligament"), is subject to tearing (a [sprain](/wiki/Sprain "Sprain")) as it is weaker than the medial ligament and it resists [inward rotation](/wiki/Inversion_(kinesiology) "Inversion (kinesiology)") of the talocrural joint.[[8]](#cite_note-Moore_2018_p.-8): 825

#### Fractures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Fractures")]

This section is an excerpt from [Ankle fracture](/wiki/Ankle_fracture "Ankle fracture").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ankle_fracture&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Bimalleolar_fracture_legend.jpg/250px-Bimalleolar_fracture_legend.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bimalleolar_fracture_legend.jpg)

Fracture of both sides of the ankle with dislocation as seen on anteroposterior X-ray. (1) fibula, (2) tibia, (arrow) medial malleolus, (arrowhead) lateral malleolus

An [ankle fracture](/wiki/Ankle_fracture "Ankle fracture") is a break of one or more of the bones that make up the [ankle joint](/wiki/Ankle_joint "Ankle joint").[[21]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_OI2019-21) Symptoms may include pain, swelling, [bruising](/wiki/Bruising "Bruising"), and an inability to walk on the injured leg.[[21]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_OI2019-21) Complications may include an associated [high ankle sprain](/wiki/High_ankle_sprain "High ankle sprain"), [compartment syndrome](/wiki/Compartment_syndrome "Compartment syndrome"), stiffness, [malunion](/wiki/Malunion "Malunion"), and [post-traumatic arthritis](/wiki/Post-traumatic_arthritis "Post-traumatic arthritis").[[21]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_OI2019-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_Stat2019-22)

Ankle fractures may result from excessive stress on the joint such as from rolling an ankle or from [blunt trauma](/wiki/Blunt_trauma "Blunt trauma").[[21]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_OI2019-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_Stat2019-22) Types of ankle fractures include lateral [malleolus](/wiki/Malleolus "Malleolus"), medial malleolus, posterior malleolus, [bimalleolar](/wiki/Bimalleolar_fracture "Bimalleolar fracture"), and [trimalleolar fractures](/wiki/Trimalleolar_fracture "Trimalleolar fracture").[[21]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_OI2019-21) The [Ottawa ankle rule](/wiki/Ottawa_ankle_rule "Ottawa ankle rule") can help determine the need for X-rays.[[22]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_Stat2019-22) Special X-ray views called stress views help determine whether an ankle fracture is unstable.

Treatment depends on the fracture type. Ankle stability largely dictates non-operative vs. operative treatment. Non-operative treatment includes splinting or casting while operative treatment includes fixing the fracture with metal implants through an open reduction internal fixation ([ORIF](/wiki/ORIF "ORIF")).[[21]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_OI2019-21) Significant recovery generally occurs within four months while completely recovery usually takes up to one year.[[21]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_OI2019-21)

Ankle fractures are common, occurring in over 1.8 per 1000 adults and 1 per 1000 children per year.[[22]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_Stat2019-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_Yeu2016-23) In North America this figure increases to more than 14 in ever 10,000 patients admitted to the Emergency Room.[[24]](#cite_note-24) They occur most commonly in young males and older females.[[22]](#cite_note-Ankle_fracture_Stat2019-22)

### Imaging

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Imaging")]

The initial evaluation of suspected ankle pathology is usually by [projectional radiography](/wiki/Projectional_radiography "Projectional radiography") ("X-ray").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/X-ray_of_frontal_tibiotalar_surface_angle_%28TTS%29.jpg/250px-X-ray_of_frontal_tibiotalar_surface_angle_%28TTS%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:X-ray_of_frontal_tibiotalar_surface_angle_(TTS).jpg)

Tibiotalar surface angle (TTS)

Varus or valgus deformity, if suspected, can be measured with the frontal tibiotalar surface angle (TTS), formed by the mid-longitudinal tibial axis (such as through a line bisecting the tibia at 8 and 13 cm above the tibial plafond) and the talar surface.[[25]](#cite_note-Nosewicz2012-25) An angle of less than 84 degrees is regarded as *talipes varus*, and an angle of more than 94 degrees is regarded as *talipes valgus*.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

For ligamentous injury, there are three main landmarks on X-rays: The first is the *tibiofibular clear space*, the horizontal distance from the lateral border of the posterior tibial malleolus to the medial border of the fibula, with greater than 5 mm being abnormal. The second is *tibiofibular overlap*, the horizontal distance between the medial border of the fibula and the lateral border of the anterior tibial prominence, with less than 10 mm being abnormal. The final measurement is the *medial clear space*, the distance between the lateral aspect of the medial malleolus and the medial border of the talus at the level of the talar dome, with a measurement greater than 4 mm being abnormal. Loss of any of these normal anatomic spaces can indirectly reflect ligamentous injury or occult fracture, and can be followed by MRI or CT.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Abnormalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Abnormalities")]

[Clubfoot](/wiki/Clubfoot "Clubfoot") or talipes equinovarus, which occurs in one to two of every 1,000 live births, involves multiple abnormalities of the foot.[[28]](#cite_note-pmid14989573-28) Equinus refers to the downard deflection of the ankle, and is named for the walking on the toes in the manner of a horse.[[29]](#cite_note-Källén2014-29) This does not occur because it is accompanied by an inward rotation of the foot ([varus deformity](/wiki/Varus_deformity "Varus deformity")), which untreated, results in walking on the sides of the feet. Treatment may involve manipulation and casting or surgery.[[28]](#cite_note-pmid14989573-28)

Ankle joint equinus, normally in adults, relates to restricted ankle joint range of motion(ROM).[[30]](#cite_note-30) Calf muscle stretching exercises are normally helpful to increase the ankle joint dorsiflexion and used to manage clinical symptoms resulting from ankle equinus.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Occasionally a human ankle has a **ball-and-socket ankle joint** and fusion of the talo-navicular joint.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ankle&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: History")]

The word ankle or ancle is common, in various forms, to [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages"), probably connected in origin with the [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") *angulus*, or [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") αγκυλος, meaning bent.[[33]](#cite_note-EB1911-33)
